blockchain.definition: A shared digital record that stores information in linked blocks across many computers.
blockchain.analogy: Like a shared Google Doc that everyone can read but nobody can secretly edit.

block.definition: A container that holds a batch of transactions, like a page in a ledger.
block.analogy: Like a page in a notebook that gets sealed once it's full.

hash.definition: A unique digital fingerprint created from data that always produces the same result for the same input.
hash.analogy: Like a fingerprint — unique to each person and impossible to fake.

consensus.definition: The process by which all participants in a network agree on what's true.
consensus.analogy: Like a group vote where the majority must agree before anything is official.

distributed-ledger.definition: A record book that's copied across thousands of computers, with no single owner.
distributed-ledger.analogy: Like having identical copies of a town's record book in every house.

cryptocurrency.definition: Digital money that uses cryptography for security and runs on a blockchain without banks.
cryptocurrency.analogy: Like digital cash that doesn't need a bank to work.

token.definition: A digital asset that represents something of value and lives on an existing blockchain.
token.analogy: Like arcade tokens — they have value within their system.

bitcoin.definition: The first cryptocurrency, created in 2009 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto.
bitcoin.analogy: Like digital gold — scarce, valuable, and not controlled by any government.

ethereum.definition: A blockchain platform that lets developers build applications and create tokens on top of it.
ethereum.analogy: Like an app store where anyone can build and run programs.

wallet.definition: A tool that stores the digital keys you need to access your cryptocurrency.
wallet.analogy: Like a keychain — it doesn't hold your money, but holds the keys to access it.

private-key.definition: A secret code that proves you own your crypto and lets you send it.
private-key.analogy: Like the PIN to your bank account — never share it with anyone.

public-key.definition: Your crypto address that others use to send you funds.
public-key.analogy: Like your email address — safe to share so others can reach you.

gas-fee.definition: The cost you pay to use a blockchain network, like a processing fee.
gas-fee.analogy: Like postage stamps — you need them to send anything through the network.

smart-contract.definition: A self-executing program stored on a blockchain that runs automatically when conditions are met.
smart-contract.analogy: Like a vending machine — put in the right amount and it automatically gives you what you selected.

nft.definition: A unique digital certificate of ownership stored on a blockchain.
nft.analogy: Like a signed certificate of authenticity for a piece of art.

defi.definition: Financial services (lending, borrowing, trading) that operate without banks or middlemen, using smart contracts instead.
defi.analogy: Like a farmer's market for money — people deal directly with each other.

mining.definition: The process of using computing power to validate transactions and add new blocks.
mining.analogy: Like solving a math puzzle — whoever solves it first gets to add the next page to the ledger and earns a reward.

staking.definition: Locking up your crypto to help validate transactions on the network and earn rewards.
staking.analogy: Like putting money in a savings account — you commit it for a period and earn interest.

decentralization.definition: A system where control is spread across many participants instead of being held by one authority.
decentralization.analogy: Like Wikipedia — thousands of editors instead of one publisher.

peer-to-peer.definition: A network where participants interact directly with each other without going through a central server.
peer-to-peer.analogy: Like passing a note directly to someone instead of sending it through a teacher.

seed-phrase.definition: A sequence of 12 to 24 words that acts as a master backup to recover your entire wallet and all its funds.
seed-phrase.analogy: Like a master key hidden under the mat — whoever has it can access everything in the house.

public-address.definition: A shareable string derived from your public key that others use to send cryptocurrency to you.
public-address.analogy: Like your home address — you can hand it out freely so people know where to send mail.

cold-storage.definition: A method of keeping private keys completely offline so they are never exposed to the internet.
cold-storage.analogy: Like storing cash in a safe buried in your backyard — secure but not connected to anything.

transaction.definition: A signed instruction recorded on a blockchain to transfer value or trigger an action.
transaction.analogy: Like writing and signing a cheque — it's a formal instruction that gets recorded permanently.

confirmation.definition: Each time a new block is added on top of the block containing your transaction, increasing its security.
confirmation.analogy: Like getting stamps on a document — more stamps mean it's more officially approved and harder to reverse.

exchange.definition: A platform where you can buy, sell, or trade cryptocurrencies, either with other users or the platform itself.
exchange.analogy: Like a currency exchange booth at the airport, but open 24/7 and available online.

liquidity.definition: How easily an asset can be bought or sold at a stable price without causing large price swings.
liquidity.analogy: Like water in a pool — a full pool lets you dive in easily, while a near-empty one means every move causes big waves.

yield-farming.definition: A strategy of earning rewards by providing liquidity or staking assets in DeFi protocols.
yield-farming.analogy: Like renting out your land to a farmer — your idle assets go to work and generate income for you.

liquidity-pool.definition: A smart contract that holds two tokens contributed by users, enabling decentralized trading between them.
liquidity-pool.analogy: Like a communal pot of two currencies that anyone can swap from, with contributors sharing the trading fees.

dao.definition: A Decentralized Autonomous Organization where rules are encoded in smart contracts and decisions are made by token holders voting.
dao.analogy: Like a company run entirely by shareholder votes, with no CEO — the code enforces whatever the majority decides.

governance-token.definition: A token that grants holders the right to vote on changes to a protocol's rules or treasury.
governance-token.analogy: Like a shareholder's voting stock — the more you hold, the more say you have in company decisions.

layer-2.definition: A secondary network built on top of a base blockchain to process transactions faster and at lower cost.
layer-2.analogy: Like an express lane on a highway — it handles most of the traffic so the main road stays clear.

oracle.definition: A service that fetches real-world data — prices, weather, sports results — and delivers it to smart contracts on a blockchain.
oracle.analogy: Like a trusted news feed plugged into a vending machine so it can charge the right price automatically.

dex.definition: A Decentralized Exchange where users trade cryptocurrencies directly with each other via smart contracts, with no company in the middle.
dex.analogy: Like a peer-to-peer marketplace for tokens — no middleman takes a cut or holds your funds.

whitepaper.definition: A technical document that outlines a blockchain project's purpose, design, and tokenomics.
whitepaper.analogy: Like a detailed business plan and blueprint combined — it explains what the project does and how it works.

market-cap.definition: The total market value of a cryptocurrency, calculated by multiplying its price by the circulating supply.
market-cap.analogy: Like a company's market valuation — it tells you the overall size of the asset relative to others.

volatility.definition: The degree to which an asset's price rises and falls sharply over a short period.
volatility.analogy: Like a roller coaster — high volatility means big, fast swings in either direction.

proof-of-work.definition: A consensus mechanism where computers compete to solve a computationally hard puzzle in order to validate a new block.
proof-of-work.analogy: Like a contest where you must show your work to win — the effort proves you played by the rules.

proof-of-stake.definition: A consensus mechanism where validators are randomly selected to confirm blocks based on how much crypto they have locked up as collateral.
proof-of-stake.analogy: Like a lottery where more tickets (staked coins) increase your chances of being chosen to run the next round.

airdrop.definition: A free distribution of tokens to wallet addresses, often used to reward early users or grow a community.
airdrop.analogy: Like a store handing out free samples to attract new customers and reward loyal ones.
